era_context: Early Bronze Age III. Egypt's Old Kingdom is collapsing; the First Intermediate Period is beginning or imminent. Ur III in Mesopotamia is consolidating power and establishing intensive administrative systems. The Indus Valley Early Harappan period is characterized by expanding settlements and developing trade networks. Bronze technology is mature across the Near East.

events.title: Approximate era of Old Kingdom collapse and First Intermediate Period transition in Egypt
events.description: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is fragmenting. Royal authority is weakening, and regional governors (nomarchs) are consolidating independent power. The centralized state apparatus that characterized the Old Kingdom is breaking down. This transition marks the beginning of the First Intermediate Period, a time of political decentralization, reduced monumental construction, and the rise of local elites. The exact year of this transition is not attested; it is assigned here by convention within the broader era of Old Kingdom decline.
key_figures: Late Dynasty 6 pharaohs (names and reigns uncertain)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of reduced monumental construction and settlement patterns

events.certainty_note: The transition from Old Kingdom to First Intermediate Period is not dated to a specific year in any source. Traditional chronology places this transition c. 2180 BCE, but this is debated. The year -2795 is assigned by convention to this era, but no specific events for this year are attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Old Kingdom collapse is highly contested. Traditional chronology places the end of Dynasty 6 and the beginning of the First Intermediate Period c. 2180 BCE, but some scholars propose dates up to 100–200 years earlier or later. No contemporary source describes the year 2795 BCE specifically. The 'collapse' was likely a gradual process of decentralization rather than a sudden event, and assigning it to a single year is a historiographic convention rather than a historical fact.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2795 BCE is negligible. No contemporary written sources describe this year. Knowledge derives from king lists (compiled centuries later), administrative archives (which do not date individual years), and archaeology (which provides broad chronological frameworks but not year-by-year precision). Egyptian chronology is heavily dependent on disputed calculations and king list lengths. The surviving record is biased toward elite, state-sponsored narratives; the experiences of common people and non-literate societies are invisible. The 'collapse' of the Old Kingdom is a modern historiographic construct; ancient sources do not describe it as a sudden event.
